summary: Missing teeth require replacement to prevent adjacent tooth migration, opposing tooth supra-eruption, and occlusal instability. Treatment options include dental implants (95-98% 10-year survival), fixed bridges (90% 10-year survival), and removable partial dentures. Single implant-supported crowns are the gold standard for individual missing teeth. Treatment planning should consider bone volume, occlusion, and patient preferences.
risk_factors: Insufficient alveolar bone for implant placement
Adjacent tooth condition and periodontal status
alternatives: Endosseous dental implant with single crown (gold standard)
Fixed dental bridge using adjacent teeth as abutments
No treatment with monitoring (third molars or non-functional teeth)
referral_summary: Implant placement is typically performed by oral surgeons or periodontists. Bone grafting may be needed if ridge resorption has occurred. Prosthodontists manage complex full-arch rehabilitation cases. Third molar absence generally requires no intervention.
patient_education: A missing tooth can cause neighboring teeth to shift over time, which may affect your bite and jaw alignment. There are several replacement options available. A dental implant is a titanium post placed in the jawbone that acts as an artificial root, topped with a natural-looking crown. A bridge uses the teeth on either side to support a replacement tooth. Your dentist will recommend the best option based on your specific situation.
